title: Breakeven demonstration of quantum low-density parity-check codes
authors: Edwin Tham, Michael L. Goldman, Shantanu Debnath, Ashay N. Patel, Jyothi Saraladevi, Jason Nguyen, Erik Nielsen, Neal Pisenti, Kenneth Wright, John Gamble, Nicolas Delfosse
abstract: High-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes are a leading candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computing. They feature higher encoding rates than planar alternatives such as the surface code, but their implementation often entails significant hardware hurdles like the need for long-range couplers. We leverage the flexibility of a trapped-ion quantum computer to demonstrate nine quantum error-correcting codes with starkly different qubit connectivity requirements on a single device without any hardware reconfiguration. These experiments span three families of quantum error-correcting codes: qLDPC codes, topological codes, and concatenated codes. With a qLDPC code encoding 4 logical qubits into 18 physical qubits, we achieve a logical error rate up to $9\times$ better than a previous demonstration of a similar code on superconducting solid-state qubits. Moreover, our implementation exhibits breakeven performance, with some instances achieving qubit lifetimes comparable to or slightly exceeding that of our trapped-ion qubits. We use a novel implementation of the optical-metastable-ground (OMG) architecture for addressable mid-circuit measurement and reset, which enables us to perform these experiments without any ion transport or dedicated coolant ions, requirements that typically consume a large fraction of the runtime or ion count of trapped-ion quantum computers.
summary: This work demonstrates the feasibility of implementing advanced quantum error correction using qLDPC codes on a trapped-ion quantum computer. By leveraging a novel OMG architecture, the researchers achieved state-of-the-art logical error suppression and demonstrated breakeven performance for multiple code types. This marks a significant step toward building fault-tolerant quantum computers.
main_problem: Demonstrating high-rate quantum error correction (QEC) using qLDPC codes to achieve fault tolerance in a physical hardware platform.
main_result: The experiment successfully demonstrated nine different QEC codes, achieving logical error rates significantly lower than previous demonstrations and showing breakeven performance where logical lifetimes approach or exceed physical qubit lifetimes.
method: Syndrome measurements are performed using controlled gates on ancilla qubits within a trapped-ion system, followed by decoding the syndrome data to estimate the logical error rate over multiple code cycles.
model_or_system: The platform is a trapped-ion quantum computer utilizing $^{133}	ext{Ba}^+$ ions. The novel Optical-Metastable-Ground (OMG) architecture enables mid-circuit measurement and reset without ion transport, allowing for the implementation of various code families.
key_observables: Logical error rate ($\epsilon_L$), logical qubit lifetime ($T_{	ext{log}}$), physical coherence times ($T_1, T_2^*$).
important_parameters: High encoding rates (e.g., qLDPC codes), $9	imes$ improvement in error suppression compared to superconducting qubits.
main_assumptions: The use of post-selection is employed for error handling, and the analysis relies on modeling noise channels based on measured gate fidelities.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the physical setup (ion trap), the sequence of operations for mid-circuit measurement (MCM), and simulated performance curves comparing logical vs. physical lifetimes under varying noise conditions.
paper_structure: The paper details the theoretical framework of qLDPC codes, describes the experimental implementation using the OMG architecture for MCM, presents measurements of error rates over multiple cycles, and concludes by analyzing lifetime scaling and fidelity improvements via remapping.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, high-fidelity demonstration of fault tolerance on trapped ions, directly addressing hardware limitations (like ion transport) that typically constrain QEC demonstrations in this modality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: A Ion trap depicted with six ions, with gates realized via counter-propagating Raman beams (green) steered from either side with acousto-optic deflectors (AOD). B An example syndrome circuit, measuring XXXX and ZZZZ stabilizers of the [[4, 2, 2]] code using two ancillae (top rails) prepared in |+⟩and measured in X. C Tanner graphs whose edges depict the highly non-trivial two-qubit gate connectivities required to implement syndrome circuits for a BB5 (left) and concatenated [[4, 2, 2]] (right) codes. D Sequence of physical operations implementing OMG-based mid-circuit measurements, with open (filled) circles representing the 0 (1) qubit state.

FIG. 2: Survival probabilities 1 −εL for the BB5 and GB4 codes versus number of syndrome cycles, for X (left) and Z (right) eigenstate memory experiments. Circles and solid curves indicate the probability that no logical fault occurred in any of the encoded logical qubits. Diamonds and dashed curves indicate the corresponding probabilities without decoding. Error bars around each point depict the 95% Wilson confidence intervals for our estimate of εL, and shaded bands show 95% confidence bands around each fitted curve.

FIG. 3: A 2D lattice on which we depict the Tanner graphs of a standard [[18, 2, 3]] (dashed square) and rotated [[16, 2, 4]] (rotated square) toric codes. Although not drawn on a toric surface, the boundaries of the solid and dashed squares are understood to wrap around along opposing edges to satisfy the toric boundary conditions.
FIG. 4: Distribution of two-qubit (MS) gate noise as measured by DRB.

FIG. 5: Simultaneous two-qubit DRB results. Estimated MS gate error rate, aggregated over 36 runs, is shown as a function of qubit pair. In each run, simultaneous DRB is performed on the sets of 5, 4, 3, and then 2 disjoint pairs separated by the vertical dashed lines. An average error rate above the mean in Fig. 4 indicates the presence of crosstalk.
